items.prompt_md: Why does `permute` check membership with a `used` set instead of checking `x in path` directly?
options: Checking `used` (a set) is O(1) per candidate, while scanning `path` (a list) would be O(n) — the set keeps each level's loop fast
Because Python lists cannot contain duplicate values
Because `path` is reset between every recursive call
Because sets preserve insertion order and lists don't
items.answer: Checking `used` (a set) is O(1) per candidate, while scanning `path` (a list) would be O(n) — the set keeps each level's loop fast
items.explanation_md: `used` mirrors what's already in `path` but as a hash set, so "is x already placed?" is O(1) instead of an O(n) scan of path repeated for every candidate at every recursion level.

items.prompt_md: You need every distinct ordering (arrangement) of a small set of unique values, where each value must appear exactly once and order matters. Which approach fits?
options: Backtrack over all n elements at every position, skipping ones already placed via a `used` set, and undo both path and used when backing out
Backtrack with an index that only ever moves forward, choosing include/exclude at each index
Sort the values, then use two pointers converging from the ends
Use a sliding window sized to the target length
items.answer: Backtrack over all n elements at every position, skipping ones already placed via a `used` set, and undo both path and used when backing out
items.explanation_md: "Every ordering, each value used exactly once, order matters" is the fingerprint for permutation backtracking with a used-set — a forward-only index (as in subsets or combination sum) can't produce arrangements where order matters.

items.prompt_md: How many leaves does the permutation backtracking search produce for n distinct elements, and what does that make the overall time complexity?
options: n! leaves, so the search is O(n * n!) once you count the O(n) cost of copying each full permutation into the result
2^n leaves, so the search is O(n * 2^n)
n^2 leaves, so the search is O(n^3)
n leaves, so the search is O(n^2)
items.answer: n! leaves, so the search is O(n * n!) once you count the O(n) cost of copying each full permutation into the result
items.explanation_md: Each of the n positions chooses among the remaining unused values, giving n! full arrangements. Recording each one costs O(n) to copy `path`, for O(n * n!) total.
